desc: Time as an occasion or instance (e.g., first time).
note: German uses 'Mal' (countable), Japanese uses 'kai' (counter), Chinese uses 'cì' (measure word), while duration uses different words.

desc: Time as a verb meaning to measure or record duration.
note: German uses 'zeitnehmen', Japanese uses 'jikan o hakaru', Chinese uses 'jìshí', distinct from the noun forms.

desc: Get meaning obtain or receive (e.g., I got a present).
note: German uses 'bekommen', Japanese uses 'morau', Chinese uses 'dédào'.

desc: Get meaning become (e.g., I got tired).
note: German uses 'werden', Japanese uses 'naru', Chinese uses 'biàn dé'.

desc: Get meaning understand (e.g., I don't get it).
note: German uses 'verstehen', Japanese uses 'wakaru', Chinese uses 'dǒng'.

desc: People as a plural noun meaning human beings in general (e.g., Many people are here).
note: German uses 'Leute' (plural), Japanese uses 'hitobito' (plural of 'hito'), Chinese uses 'rénmen' (plural suffix).

desc: People as a countable noun meaning a nation or ethnic group (e.g., The French people).
note: German uses 'Volk', Japanese uses 'minzoku' or 'kokumin', Chinese uses 'mínzú'.

desc: First as an ordinal adjective (before others) and as an adverb (before anything else) (e.g., my first car; first, let's eat).
note: German uses 'erste' for adjective and 'zuerst' for adverb; Japanese uses 'daiichi' / 'ichiban' and 'saisho ni'; Chinese uses 'dì yī' and 'shǒuxiān'.

desc: Know meaning to have factual knowledge (e.g., I know the answer).
note: German uses 'wissen', Japanese uses 'shiru', Chinese uses 'zhīdào'.

desc: Know meaning to be acquainted with a person (e.g., I know her).
note: German uses 'kennen', Japanese uses 'shiru' (same), Chinese uses 'rènshi' — German and Chinese differentiate from factual knowledge.

desc: Know meaning to know how to do something (e.g., I know how to swim).
note: German uses 'können', Japanese uses 'dekiru', Chinese uses 'huì'.

desc: See meaning to perceive with eyes (e.g., I see a bird).
note: German uses 'sehen', Japanese uses 'miru', Chinese uses 'kàn'.

desc: See meaning to understand (e.g., I see what you mean).
note: German uses 'verstehen', Japanese uses 'wakaru', Chinese uses 'míngbái'.

desc: See meaning to meet or encounter (e.g., I saw him yesterday).
note: German uses 'treffen', Japanese uses 'au', Chinese uses 'jiàn'.

desc: Make meaning to create or construct (e.g., She made a cake).
note: German uses 'machen', Japanese uses 'tsukuru', Chinese uses 'zuò'.

desc: Make meaning to cause a change of state (e.g., It made me happy).
note: German uses 'machen' (same), Japanese uses 'saseru' (causative), Chinese uses 'ràng'.

desc: Make meaning to force or compel (e.g., They made him leave).
note: German uses 'zwingen' or 'lassen', Japanese uses 'shīru', Chinese uses 'bī'.

desc: Think meaning to hold an opinion or believe (e.g., I think it's a good idea).
note: German uses 'glauben' or 'denken', Japanese uses 'omou', Chinese uses 'rènwéi'.

desc: Think meaning to use one's mind to consider (e.g., I need to think about it).
note: German uses 'denken', Japanese uses 'kangaeru', Chinese uses 'xiǎng'.

desc: Think meaning to intend or plan (e.g., I think I'll go now).
note: German uses 'denken' (same), Japanese uses 'tsumorida', Chinese uses 'dǎsuàn'.

desc: Back as the rear part of the human body (e.g., My back hurts).
note: German uses 'Rücken', Japanese uses 'senaka', Chinese uses 'bèi'.

desc: Back as the rear part of an object or location (e.g., The back of the house).
note: German uses 'Rückseite' or 'hinten', Japanese uses 'ushiro', Chinese uses 'hòumiàn'.

desc: Back as a verb meaning to reverse or move backwards (e.g., He backed the car into the garage).
note: German uses 'zurücksetzen', Japanese uses 'modosu' or 'bakkusuru', Chinese uses 'dào chē'.

desc: Want meaning to desire an object (e.g., I want a new phone).
note: German uses 'wollen', Japanese uses 'hoshii' (adjective), Chinese uses 'yào'.

desc: Want meaning to desire to do an action (e.g., I want to go home).
note: German uses 'wollen' (same), Japanese uses the '-tai' form, Chinese uses 'xiǎng'.

desc: Want meaning to need or should (e.g., You want to be careful).
note: German uses 'sollen', Japanese uses 'beki' or 'shinai to', Chinese uses 'yīnggāi'.

desc: Go meaning to move or travel to a place (e.g., I go to school).
note: German uses 'gehen', Japanese uses 'iku', Chinese uses 'qù'.

desc: Go meaning to leave or depart (e.g., I have to go now).
note: German uses 'weggehen' or 'gehen', Japanese uses 'deru' or 'saru', Chinese uses 'zǒu'.

desc: Go meaning to become or change state (e.g., The milk went sour).
note: German uses 'werden', Japanese uses 'naru', Chinese uses 'biàn'.

desc: Well as an adjective meaning in good health and as an adverb meaning in a good manner (e.g., I am well; She sings well).
note: German uses 'gesund' (adj) and 'gut' (adv); Japanese uses 'genki' and 'yoku'; Chinese uses 'hǎo' for both.

desc: Well as a noun meaning a deep hole for water (e.g., We dug a well).
note: German uses 'Brunnen', Japanese uses 'ido', Chinese uses 'jǐng'.

desc: Said as the past tense of 'say' (e.g., He said hello).
note: German uses 'sagte', Japanese uses 'itta', Chinese uses 'shuōle'.

desc: Said as an adjective meaning previously mentioned (e.g., The said person is here).
note: German uses 'besagt' or 'genannt', Japanese uses 'iwareru' or 'dō', Chinese uses 'suǒ shuō de'.

desc: Way meaning a method or manner (e.g., Show me the way to do it).
note: German uses 'Methode' or 'Weise', Japanese uses 'hōhō', Chinese uses 'fāngfǎ'.

desc: Way meaning a path or route (e.g., Is this the way home?).
note: German uses 'Weg', Japanese uses 'michi', Chinese uses 'lù'.

desc: Way meaning a characteristic or habit (e.g., That's just his way).
note: German uses 'Art', Japanese uses 'shūkan' or 'kuse', Chinese uses 'xíguàn'.

desc: Even as an adverb meaning 'still' or 'including' and as an adjective meaning level or equal (e.g., He even won; The surface is even).
note: German uses 'sogar' (adv) and 'eben' (adj); Japanese uses 'sae' (adv) and 'taira' (adj); Chinese uses 'shènzhì' (adv) and 'píng' (adj).

desc: Even as a verb meaning to make level or equal (e.g., They evened the score).
note: German uses 'ebnen', Japanese uses 'tairagu', Chinese uses 'nòng píng'.

desc: To make something correct or straighten it.
note: Unlike the adjective sense, German uses 'korrigieren' or 'wiedergutmachen', Japanese '正す', Mandarin '纠正'.

desc: A moral or legal entitlement.
note: German 'Recht', Japanese '権利', Mandarin '权利' are distinct from the correct sense.

desc: Accurate or correctly done; also used as an adverb meaning correctly.
note: German uses 'richtig' for both, but Japanese and Mandarin distinguish adjective and adverb with affixes.

desc: To perform labor or be employed.
note: German 'arbeiten', Japanese '働く', Mandarin '工作' are different from the function sense.

desc: To operate or function correctly.
note: German 'funktionieren', Japanese '動く', Mandarin '运作' are distinct from the labor sense.

desc: A piece of creative output, such as a book or painting.
note: German 'Werk', Japanese '作品', Mandarin '作品' are different from the verb senses.

desc: To grasp or seize with the hand.
note: German 'nehmen', Japanese '取る', Mandarin '拿' are used for physical grasp, distinct from time or consumption senses.

desc: To require a certain amount of time.
note: German 'dauern', Japanese 'かかる', Mandarin '花' are used for duration, distinct from grasp.

desc: To consume or ingest, especially medicine.
note: German 'einnehmen', Japanese '飲む', Mandarin '服用' are specific to consumption.

desc: A continuous recording of a scene in film or music.
note: German 'Aufnahme', Japanese 'テイク', Mandarin '镜头' are different from verb senses.

desc: To obtain or receive something (simple past).
note: German 'bekam', Japanese 'もらった', Mandarin '得到' differ from the possession sense.

desc: To have or possess (using 'have got' construction).
note: German 'haben', Japanese '持っている', Mandarin '有' are used for possession, contrasting with obtain.

desc: To understand (past tense of 'get' meaning understand).
note: German 'kapierte', Japanese 'わかった', Mandarin '懂了' differ from obtain and possess.

desc: The biological existence of a living being.
note: German 'Leben', Japanese '生命', Mandarin '生命' contrast with lifestyle sense.

desc: A particular way of living or daily activity.
note: German 'Leben' (same), Japanese '生活', Mandarin '生活' differ from biological life.

desc: A written account of someone's life (biography).
note: German 'Biographie', Japanese '伝記', Mandarin '传记' are distinct from other life senses.

desc: To utter words or express in speech.
note: German 'sagen', Japanese '言う', Mandarin '说' differ from the indicating sense.

desc: To indicate or show a value (e.g., a clock).
note: German 'zeigen', Japanese '指す', Mandarin '显示' contrast with speaking.

desc: A right or opportunity to express an opinion.
note: German 'Mitspracherecht', Japanese '発言権', Mandarin '发言权' are distinct noun phrases.

desc: Very good or excellent.
note: German 'großartig', Japanese 'すごい', Mandarin '棒' differ from the noun sense.

desc: A person who is outstanding in their field (plural: the greats).
note: German 'die Großen', Japanese '偉人', Mandarin '伟人' are different from the adjective.

desc: Final in a sequence; also used as an adverb meaning at the end.
note: German 'letzte', Japanese '最後の', Mandarin '最后的' contrast with the verb sense.

desc: To continue for a period of time.
note: German 'dauern', Japanese '続く', Mandarin '持续' differ from the adjective.

desc: The one that comes at the end (e.g., the last of something).
note: German 'der/die/das Letzte', Japanese '最後のもの', Mandarin '最后一个' are distinct from the adjective.

desc: Of the highest quality; also used as an adverb meaning to the highest degree.
note: German 'beste/am besten', Japanese '一番良い/一番', Mandarin '最好的/最' show adverbial difference.

desc: To defeat or outdo someone.
note: German 'übertreffen', Japanese '打ち負かす', Mandarin '击败' are distinct from the adjective.

desc: To have deep affection for someone.
note: German 'lieben', Japanese '愛する', Mandarin '爱' differ from the noun sense.

desc: A person who is loved or strong affection (plural: loves).
note: German 'Liebe/Liebling', Japanese '愛する人', Mandarin '爱人' contrast with verb.

desc: To provide staff for a position or operation.
note: German 'bemannen', Japanese '配置する', Mandarin '操纵' differ from the noun.

desc: An adult male human (plural: men).
note: German 'Mann', Japanese '男', Mandarin '男人' are standard nouns.

desc: A place where one lives (plural: homes).
note: German 'Zuhause', Japanese '家', Mandarin '家' differ from the verb.

desc: To direct or guide towards a target (e.g., a missile).
note: German 'ansteuern', Japanese '狙う', Mandarin '锁定' differ from the noun.

desc: Extending for a long period of time; also used as an adverb meaning for a long time.
note: German 'lang/lange', Japanese '長い/長く', Mandarin '长/久' show adjective-adverb split.

desc: To desire or yearn for something.
note: German 'sich sehnen', Japanese '憧れる', Mandarin '渴望' are completely different from the time sense.

desc: To direct one's gaze at something.
note: German 'schauen', Japanese '見る', Mandarin '看' differ from the appear sense.

desc: To seem or appear in a certain way.
note: German 'aussehen', Japanese '見える', Mandarin '看起来' contrast with looking.

desc: An expression or appearance of a person (plural: looks).
note: German 'Aussehen', Japanese '見た目', Mandarin '样子' are distinct from verb senses.

desc: The final point or extremity of something.
note: English uses 'end' for both a physical endpoint and a temporal conclusion, but German uses 'Ende' for the noun while Japanese uses 'owari' and Mandarin uses '尽头' or '终点', contrasting with the verb form.

desc: To bring something to a conclusion.
note: English uses 'end' as a verb meaning to finish, while German uses 'beenden', Japanese uses 'owaru', and Mandarin uses '结束', showing a clear verb-noun split.

desc: Past tense of 'find' meaning to discover something.
note: English uses 'found' as the past of 'find', but German uses 'fand', Japanese uses 'mitsuketa', and Mandarin uses '找到', distinct from the homograph meaning 'establish'.

desc: To establish or set up an institution or organization.
note: English uses 'found' to mean 'establish', while German uses 'gründete', Japanese uses 'setsuritsu shita', and Mandarin uses '创立', contrasting with the past tense of 'find'.

desc: To display or present something to someone.
note: English 'show' as a verb meaning 'display' is translated as German 'zeigen', Japanese 'miseru', and Mandarin '展示', distinct from the noun and phrasal verb senses.

desc: A performance or programme for entertainment.
note: English 'show' as a noun is German 'Sendung' or 'Show', Japanese 'shō', and Mandarin '节目', contrasting with the verb sense.

desc: To appear or arrive (show up).
note: The phrasal verb 'show up' meaning 'appear' is German 'auftauchen', Japanese 'arawareru', and Mandarin '出现', showing a different lexical choice from 'display'.

desc: Large in size or extent.
note: English 'big' meaning large is German 'groß', Japanese 'ōkii', and Mandarin '大', distinct from the sense of importance.

desc: Significant or important.
note: English 'big' meaning important is German 'wichtig', Japanese 'daiji', and Mandarin '重要', contrasting with size.

desc: Older, especially in a sibling relationship.
note: English 'big' in 'big sister' refers to older, while German uses 'ältere', Japanese uses 'ane' (older sister), and Mandarin uses '姐姐', a distinct translation from size/importance.

desc: To touch or explore by touch.
note: English 'feel' meaning to touch is German 'fühlen', Japanese 'sawaru', and Mandarin '摸', contrasting with emotional and opinion uses.

desc: To experience an emotion or physical sensation.
note: English 'feel' for emotions is German 'fühlen', Japanese 'kanjiru', and Mandarin '感觉', but Japanese distinguishes this from tactile 'sawaru'.

desc: To hold an opinion or believe (I feel that...).
note: English 'feel' for opinion translates to German 'finden', Japanese 'omou', and Mandarin '觉得', distinct from emotion/touch senses.

desc: Certain or confident about something.
note: English 'sure' as an adjective meaning certain is German 'sicher', Japanese 'tashika da', and Mandarin '确定', contrasting with the interjection sense.

desc: An expression of agreement or affirmation (Sure!).
note: English 'sure' used as an interjection is German 'klar' or 'sicher', Japanese 'hai' or 'iidesu', and Mandarin '当然', showing a different usage from the adjective.

desc: A group of people working or playing together.
note: English 'team' as a noun is German 'Team', Japanese 'chīmu', and Mandarin '队伍', distinct from the verb form.

desc: To join together as a group (team up).
note: English 'team' as a verb (team up) is German 'sich zusammentun', Japanese 'chīmu o kumu', and Mandarin '组队', contrasting with the noun sense.

desc: Immediate relatives, typically parents and children.
note: English 'family' for immediate household is German 'Familie', Japanese 'kazoku', and Mandarin '家人', distinct from extended relatives.

desc: Extended relatives or all one's kin.
note: English 'family' can mean relatives in general, but German uses 'Verwandte', Japanese 'shinseki', and Mandarin '亲戚', showing a split between immediate and extended.

desc: A taxonomic group in biology (e.g., cat family).
note: English 'family' in taxonomy is German 'Familie', Japanese 'ka', and Mandarin '科', a specialized sense that differs from human family terms.

desc: To retain possession of something.
note: English 'keep' meaning to retain is German 'behalten', Japanese 'motteiru', and Mandarin '保留', contrasting with the continuous action sense.

desc: To continue doing something (keep + gerund).
note: English 'keep' meaning to continue is German 'weitermachen', Japanese 'tsuzukeru', and Mandarin '继续', distinct from retaining.

desc: To fulfill or adhere to (e.g., a promise).
note: English 'keep' as in 'keep a promise' is German 'halten', Japanese 'mamoru', and Mandarin '遵守', a separate meaning from retain/continue.

desc: To place something in a location.
note: English 'put' meaning to place is German 'legen/stellen', Japanese 'oku', and Mandarin '放', contrasting with phrasal verbs.

desc: To dress oneself in clothing (put on).
note: English 'put on' means to don clothes, translated as German 'anziehen', Japanese 'kiru', and Mandarin '穿', distinct from placing.

desc: To postpone something (put off).
note: English 'put off' means to delay, rendered as German 'verschieben', Japanese 'nobasu', and Mandarin '推迟', showing a third distinct meaning.

desc: Without cost or payment.
note: English 'free' meaning gratis is German 'kostenlos', Japanese 'tada', and Mandarin '免费', distinct from liberty or availability.

desc: Having liberty or not restricted.
note: English 'free' meaning unrestricted is German 'frei', Japanese 'jiyū', and Mandarin '自由', contrasting with gratis.

desc: Not occupied or available (e.g., free time).
note: English 'free' meaning available is German 'frei' (same as liberty), but Japanese uses 'aite iru' and Mandarin uses '有空', showing a split from liberty in Japanese/Mandarin.

desc: A unit of time equal to 1/60 of a minute.
note: English 'second' as a time unit is German 'Sekunde', Japanese 'byō', and Mandarin '秒', contrasting with ordinal and verb senses.

desc: Coming after the first in order or rank.
note: English 'second' as an ordinal is German 'zweite', Japanese 'niban', and Mandarin '第二', distinct from the time unit.

desc: To formally support a proposal or motion.
note: English 'second' as a verb meaning to support is German 'unterstützen', Japanese 'sansei suru', and Mandarin '支持', a different part of speech and meaning.

desc: Past tense of 'leave' meaning to go away from.
note: English 'left' as past of leave is German 'verließ', Japanese 'deta' (e.g., 'ofisu o deta'), and Mandarin '离开了', contrasting with direction and remaining.

desc: The direction opposite of right.
note: English 'left' meaning the left side is German 'links', Japanese 'hidari', and Mandarin '左', distinct from the verb and adjective senses.

desc: Remaining or not used up.
note: English 'left' meaning remaining is German 'übrig', Japanese 'nokotte iru', and Mandarin '剩下', showing a third distinct translation.

desc: A numeral or identifying code (e.g., phone number).
note: English 'number' as an identifier is German 'Nummer', Japanese 'bangō', and Mandarin '号码', contrasting with quantity and verb.

desc: An amount or quantity of things.
note: English 'number' meaning quantity is German 'Anzahl', Japanese 'kazu', and Mandarin '数量', distinct from identifier.

desc: To assign numbers or count something.
note: English 'number' as a verb is German 'nummerieren', Japanese 'bangō o fureru', and Mandarin '编号', a different word from the noun senses.

desc: A large amount or quantity (often with 'a' or 'lots').
note: English 'lot' meaning a large amount is German 'viel', Japanese 'takusan', and Mandarin '很多', distinct from land or fate.

desc: A piece of land, often for building.
note: English 'lot' as a land parcel is German 'Grundstück', Japanese 'kichi', and Mandarin '地块', contrasting with quantity.

desc: One's fate or destiny in life.
note: English 'lot' meaning fate is German 'Los', Japanese 'unmei', and Mandarin '命运', a third distinct meaning.

desc: A word or set of words by which a person or thing is known.
note: English 'name' as an identifier is German 'Name', Japanese 'namae', and Mandarin '名字', contrasting with verb and reputation.

desc: To give a name to someone or something.
note: English 'name' as a verb is German 'nennen', Japanese 'nazukeru', and Mandarin '命名', distinct from the noun.

desc: Reputation or renown (make a name for oneself).
note: English 'name' meaning reputation is German 'Ruf', Japanese 'hyōban', and Mandarin '名声', a separate sense from identifier.

desc: To engage in fun or recreational activities.
note: English 'play' meaning to have fun is German 'spielen', Japanese 'asobu', and Mandarin '玩', contrasting with musical or theatrical senses.

desc: To perform on a musical instrument.
note: English 'play' meaning to perform music is German 'spielen' (same as recreation), but Japanese uses 'hiku' (for string/keyboard) and Mandarin uses '演奏', showing a split.

desc: To act a role in a performance.
note: English 'play' meaning to act a part is German 'spielen', Japanese 'enjiru', and Mandarin '扮演', distinct from recreation and instrument.

desc: A theatrical performance or drama.
note: English 'play' as a noun is German 'Theaterstück', Japanese 'geki', and Mandarin '话剧', contrasting with all verb senses.

desc: To employ something for a purpose (e.g., 'I use a pen to write.').
note: German 'benutzen' vs. 'verwenden' differ by formality and object type; Mandarin 'yòng' vs. 'shǐyòng' differ in register; Japanese 'tsukau' covers both, but 'mochiiru' is more formal.

desc: To consume or deplete a resource (e.g., 'The car uses a lot of gas.').
note: German 'verbrauchen' is distinct from 'benutzen'; Mandarin 'hào' or 'xiāohào'; Japanese 'tsukau' can overlap but 'shōhi suru' is specific to consumption.

desc: Secondhand, not new (e.g., 'I bought a used car.').
note: German 'gebraucht'; Mandarin 'èrshǒu'; Japanese 'chūko' — all distinct from the verb 'use'.

desc: Accustomed to something (e.g., 'I am used to the cold.').
note: German 'gewohnt sein'; Mandarin 'xíguàn'; Japanese 'narete iru' — this is a stative construction, not a simple adjective.

desc: Fabricated, not true (e.g., 'Don't believe his made-up story.').
note: German 'erfunden'; Mandarin 'biānzào de'; Japanese 'tsukuri no' — all distinct from the literal 'made up' (assembled).

desc: Wearing cosmetics (e.g., 'She is heavily made-up for the party.').
note: German 'geschminkt'; Mandarin 'huàzhuāng de'; Japanese 'keshō shita' — completely different lexical root from 'fabricated'.

desc: To move toward the speaker (e.g., 'Come here, please.').
note: German 'kommen'; Mandarin 'lái'; Japanese 'kuru' — but note German also uses 'kommen' for 'go' to the listener's location, unlike English.

desc: To originate from a place (e.g., 'She comes from Canada.').
note: German 'kommen aus'; Mandarin 'lái zì'; Japanese 'shusshin da' — Japanese uses a copular noun phrase, not a motion verb.

desc: A piece or section of a whole (e.g., 'This part of the machine is broken.').
note: German 'Teil'; Mandarin 'bùfèn'; Japanese 'bubun' — all are countable nouns.

desc: To separate or leave (e.g., 'We parted ways after lunch.').
note: German 'sich trennen'; Mandarin 'fēnbié'; Japanese 'wakareru' — verb vs. noun is a major cross-linguistic split.

desc: A political division (e.g., 'She lives in the state of Texas.').
note: German 'Bundesstaat'; Mandarin 'zhōu'; Japanese 'shū' — all are nouns, but German uses a compound.

desc: A condition or mode of being (e.g., 'He is in a state of shock.').
note: German 'Zustand'; Mandarin 'zhuàngtài'; Japanese 'jōtai' — completely different word from the political 'state'.

desc: The number 3 (e.g., 'I have three apples.').
note: German 'drei'; Mandarin 'sān'; Japanese 'san' — but Japanese also uses 'mitsu' as a native counter.

desc: Comparative of good (e.g., 'This is better than that.').
note: German 'besser'; Mandarin 'gèng hǎo'; Japanese 'yori ii' — all have irregular comparative forms.

desc: To improve or surpass (e.g., 'He bettered his own record.').
note: German 'verbessern'; Mandarin 'gǎishàn'; Japanese 'yokusuru' — verb vs. adjective/adverb is a key split.

desc: To discover something sought (e.g., 'I found my keys.').
note: German 'finden'; Mandarin 'zhǎodào'; Japanese 'mitsukeru' — note Japanese uses resultative form.

desc: To consider or judge (e.g., 'I find that movie boring.').
note: German 'finden' can also mean 'consider'; Mandarin 'juéde'; Japanese 'omou' — English uses same verb, but Mandarin and Japanese use different verbs.

desc: To assist someone (e.g., 'Can you help me?').
note: German 'helfen'; Mandarin 'bāngzhù'; Japanese 'tetsudau' — but note 'helping' physically vs. emotionally can split in Japanese ('tasukeru').

desc: To be useful (e.g., 'This map helps a lot.').
note: German 'helfen'; Mandarin 'yǒuyòng'; Japanese 'yakunitatsu' — Japanese uses a different verb for 'be useful' vs. 'assist'.

desc: Tall, elevated (e.g., 'The mountain is very high.').
note: German 'hoch'; Mandarin 'gāo'; Japanese 'takai' — standard adjective.

desc: Drunk or under influence (e.g., 'He got high on caffeine.').
note: German 'high' (borrowing); Mandarin 'gāo' doesn't work; Japanese 'hai ni naru' — different vocabulary in Mandarin ('zuì' for drunk, 'gāo' only for height).

desc: Aged, advanced in years (e.g., 'My grandmother is very old.').
note: German 'alt'; Mandarin 'lǎo'; Japanese 'toshi no totta' (people) vs. 'furui' (things) — Japanese splits human vs. object age.

desc: Not new, antique (e.g., 'This is an old book.').
note: German 'alt' (same word); Mandarin 'jiù'; Japanese 'furui' — English uses same word, but Mandarin and Japanese have distinct words.

desc: An activity for fun or sport (e.g., 'Let's play a game.').
note: German 'Spiel'; Mandarin 'yóuxì'; Japanese 'asobi' or 'gēmu' — Japanese borrows 'gēmu' for electronic games.

desc: Wild animals hunted for meat (e.g., 'Hunters tracked the game.').
note: German 'Wild'; Mandarin 'lièwù'; Japanese 'emono' — completely different root from the fun activity.

desc: An object, entity (e.g., 'What is that thing?').
note: German 'Ding'; Mandarin 'dōngxi'; Japanese 'mono' — all are generic nouns.

desc: To hand over freely (e.g., 'Give me the book.').
note: German 'geben'; Mandarin 'gěi'; Japanese 'ageru' — but Japanese has 'kureru' for when the giver is not the speaker.

desc: To cause or produce (e.g., 'The sun gives light.').
note: German 'geben' also works; Mandarin 'fāchū'; Japanese 'ataeru' — Mandarin uses a compound verb; Japanese uses a different verb.

desc: A building for living (e.g., 'We live in a big house.').
note: German 'Haus'; Mandarin 'fángzi'; Japanese 'ie' — all standard nouns.

desc: To provide space (e.g., 'This box houses the electronics.').
note: German 'beherbergen'; Mandarin 'rúngnà'; Japanese 'irete iru' — verb vs. noun is a clear cross-linguistic split.

desc: A location (e.g., 'This is a nice place.').
note: German 'Ort'; Mandarin 'dìfang'; Japanese 'basho' — all nouns.

desc: To put something somewhere (e.g., 'Place the cup on the table.').
note: German 'stellen/legen'; Mandarin 'fàng'; Japanese 'oku' — verb vs. noun; German even splits by orientation.

desc: An educational institution (e.g., 'She goes to school.').
note: German 'Schule'; Mandarin 'xuéxiào'; Japanese 'gakkō' — all standard.

desc: A group of fish (e.g., 'A school of fish swam by.').
note: German 'Schwarm'; Mandarin 'qún'; Japanese 'mure' — completely different root from educational school.

desc: Immediately following in order (e.g., 'I will see you next week.').
note: German 'nächste'; Mandarin 'xià'; Japanese 'tsugi no' — all adjectives.

desc: Nearest in space (e.g., 'The next house is blue.').
note: German 'nächste'; Mandarin 'gé bì de'; Japanese 'tonari no' — English uses same word, but Mandarin and Japanese distinguish 'next in time' vs. 'adjacent'.

desc: State of being comfortable, healthy, or happy (e.g., 'Exercise improves well-being.').
note: German 'Wohlbefinden'; Mandarin 'fúlì' or 'jiànkāng'; Japanese 'kenkō' or 'kōfuku' — often expressed as a compound or abstract noun.

desc: To have a home in a particular place.
note: German uses 'wohnen' for residing, Japanese '住む', Mandarin '住', all distinct from the verb for being alive.

desc: To exist as a living being.
note: German uses 'leben', Japanese '生きる', Mandarin '活', separate from the verb for residing.

desc: Broadcast or performed at the time of occurrence, not recorded.
note: German uses 'live' (loanword) or 'Live-', Japanese 'ライブ', Mandarin '直播', all different from the adjective for alive ('lebendig', '生きた', '活的').

desc: To produce or construct something tangible.
note: German uses 'machen', Japanese '作る', Mandarin '做' for physical creation; contrast with earning where German uses 'verdienen' and Japanese '稼ぐ'.

desc: To gain money or profit.
note: German 'verdienen', Japanese '稼ぐ', Mandarin '赚' show that English uses the same verb for creating and earning, but German and Japanese have distinct verbs.

desc: The sharp end of an object.
note: German 'Spitze', Japanese '先端', Mandarin '尖' differ from the word for a location or score.

desc: A specific place or location.
note: German 'Punkt', Japanese '点', Mandarin '点' – note that Mandarin uses the same word for location and idea but a different word for score.

desc: A unit used for scoring in games or tests.
note: German 'Punkt', Japanese '点', Mandarin '分' – Mandarin splits score from location, while German and Japanese use the same word.

desc: To look at and understand written or printed words.
note: German 'lesen', Japanese '読む', Mandarin '读' – the basic meaning.

desc: To read something aloud.
note: German uses 'vorlesen', Japanese '朗読する', Mandarin '朗读', distinct from silent reading, showing a lexical split.

desc: To narrate or relate a story.
note: German 'erzählen', Japanese '語る', Mandarin '讲' differ from the verb for giving information.

desc: To give information to someone.
note: German 'sagen', Japanese '教える', Mandarin '告诉' – English uses the same word for narrating and informing, but German and Japanese use different verbs.

desc: Of poor quality or low standard.
note: German 'schlecht', Japanese '悪い', Mandarin '差' – note Japanese uses the same word for moral badness, but German and Mandarin split.

desc: Lacking skill or ability.
note: German uses 'schlecht' (same as quality), Japanese uses '下手', and Mandarin uses '差' (same) – Japanese clearly distinguishes 'unskilled' from 'poor quality'.

desc: Solid and firm to the touch; not soft.
note: German 'hart', Japanese '硬い', Mandarin '硬' – distinct from 'difficult'.

desc: Requiring a lot of effort; difficult.
note: German 'schwierig', Japanese '難しい', Mandarin '难' – different from physical hardness.

desc: With a great deal of effort or intensity.
note: German uses 'hart' (same as physical), Japanese uses '一生懸命', Mandarin '努力' – English uses 'hard' for both physical and effort, but Japanese splits.

desc: To have a particular meaning or intention.
note: German 'bedeuten', Japanese '意味する', Mandarin '意思是' – verb sense.

desc: Unkind, cruel, or malicious.
note: German 'gemein', Japanese '意地悪', Mandarin '刻薄' – adjective sense distinct from verb.

desc: The average value of a set of numbers.
note: German 'Mittelwert', Japanese '平均', Mandarin '平均数' – noun sense, showing that English uses the same word for three unrelated concepts.

desc: To give encouragement or backing to someone or something.
note: German 'unterstützen', Japanese '支持する', Mandarin '支持' – emotional/ideological support.

desc: To hold something physically from underneath or to bear weight.
note: German 'stützen', Japanese '支える', Mandarin '支撑' – physical support, distinct from the emotional verb in all three languages.

desc: To give information verbally to someone.
note: German 'sagen', Japanese '教える', Mandarin '告诉' – similar to 'told' inform frame.

desc: To narrate or relate a story or event.
note: German 'erzählen', Japanese '語る', Mandarin '讲' – distinct from informing.

desc: Electrical energy supplied to homes and businesses.
note: German 'Strom', Japanese '電力', Mandarin '电力' – distinct from political power.

desc: The ability to control people or events; authority.
note: German 'Macht', Japanese '権力', Mandarin '权力' – political power.

desc: Physical strength or force.
note: German 'Kraft', Japanese '力', Mandarin '力量' – physical power.

desc: Perceived with the eyes; viewed.
note: German 'gesehen', Japanese '見た', Mandarin '看过' – visual perception.

desc: Encountered or met someone (often in social contexts).
note: German 'getroffen', Japanese '会った', Mandarin '见过' – distinct from visual perception, showing a split in all three languages.

desc: To cease moving or come to a halt.
note: German 'anhalten', Japanese '止まる', Mandarin '停' – movement halt.

desc: To cease an activity or action.
note: German 'aufhören', Japanese 'やめる', Mandarin '停止' or '别' – activity cessation, different from movement.

desc: A designated place where buses or trains pick up passengers.
note: German 'Haltestelle', Japanese '停留所', Mandarin '站' – noun form distinct from verb.

desc: To pour water on plants or soil.
note: German 'gießen', Japanese '水をやる', Mandarin '浇水' – verb form distinct from the noun for the liquid itself.

desc: To contact someone by telephone.
note: German 'anrufen', Japanese '電話する', Mandarin '打电话' – telephone call.

desc: To shout or cry out to attract attention.
note: German 'rufen', Japanese '呼ぶ', Mandarin '喊' or '叫' – shouting.

desc: To give a name to someone or something.
note: German 'nennen', Japanese '名付ける', Mandarin '叫' – naming.

desc: The upper part of the human body containing the brain, eyes, mouth, etc.
note: German 'Kopf', Japanese '頭', Mandarin '头' – body part.

desc: The person in charge or leader of a group.
note: German 'Leiter', Japanese '長', Mandarin '领导' – leader, distinct from body part.

desc: To move in a specified direction.
note: German 'sich begeben' or 'gehen', Japanese '向かう', Mandarin '朝…走' – verb sense, different from noun forms.

desc: To allow someone to use a property in exchange for payment
note: The rental sense of 'let' is distinct from its permission sense; German uses 'vermieten', Japanese 'kasu', Mandarin 'chūzū' for rental, while permission uses different verbs.

desc: Genuine, not fake
note: English uses 'real' for both genuine and property-related senses; German uses 'echt' vs 'Immobilien', Japanese 'honmono no' vs 'fudōsan', Mandarin 'zhēn' vs 'fángdìchǎn'.

desc: Relating to property in the phrase 'real estate'
note: The compound 'real estate' uses a different word in each target language compared to the genuine sense.

desc: Not the same, dissimilar
note: English uses 'different' for both dissimilar and alternative; German uses 'anders' vs 'ander-', Japanese 'chigau' vs 'betsu no', Mandarin 'bùtóng' vs 'biéde'.

desc: Other, alternative
note: The sense of 'different' meaning 'another' is expressed with a different word in German, Japanese, and Mandarin.

desc: To put something in a specified position
note: English 'set' covers placing, establishing, and a collection; German uses 'stellen/legen' vs 'festlegen' vs 'Satz', Japanese 'oku' vs 'kimeru' vs 'setto', Mandarin 'fàng' vs 'shèdìng' vs 'tào'.

desc: To establish or decide
note: Distinct from the placing sense, each language uses a different verb for establishing.

desc: A group of related items
note: The noun sense is expressed with a different word from the verb senses in all three languages.

desc: An idea or opinion
note: English uses 'thought' for both the noun (idea) and the verb (past tense); German uses 'Gedanke' vs 'dachte', Japanese 'omoide'/'kangae' vs 'omotta', Mandarin 'xiǎngfǎ' vs 'xiǎng'.

desc: Past tense of the verb 'think'
note: The verb form 'thought' is a different word class; it is expressed with a distinct word in German, Japanese, and Mandarin.

desc: Becoming, as in changing state
note: The 'becoming' sense of 'getting' (e.g., getting cold) uses different verbs than the 'receiving' sense; German 'werden', Japanese 'naru', Mandarin 'biàn'.

desc: Receiving or obtaining
note: For 'getting a gift', languages use verbs like German 'bekommen', Japanese 'morau', Mandarin 'dédào'.

desc: A set of people or things considered together
note: English uses 'group' as both noun and verb; German uses 'Gruppe' (noun) vs 'gruppieren' (verb), Japanese 'gurūpu' vs 'gurūpu ni suru', Mandarin 'zǔ' vs 'zǔhé'.

desc: To arrange into a group or categories
note: The verb sense is expressed with a different word from the noun sense in each language.

desc: Directing one's eyes to see something
note: The active viewing sense of 'looking' (look at) uses different verbs from the appearance sense; German 'schauen', Japanese 'miru', Mandarin 'kàn'.

desc: Seeming or appearing in a certain way
note: For 'looking tired', languages use verbs like German 'aussehen', Japanese 'mienai' (or construction), Mandarin 'kànqǐlái'.

desc: Relating to the community or the people as a whole (adjective)
note: English uses 'public' for both the adjective and the noun; German uses 'öffentlich' vs 'Öffentlichkeit', Japanese 'kōkyō' vs 'kōshū', Mandarin 'gōnggòng' vs 'gōngzhòng'.

desc: The general population (noun)
note: The noun form is a different word from the adjective in each target language.

desc: The highest part or point of something
note: English 'top' functions as noun, adjective, and verb; German uses 'Spitze' (noun), 'obere' (adj), 'übertreffen' (verb); Japanese 'chōjō', 'ichiban', 'nobiru'; Mandarin 'dǐng', 'zuìgāo', 'chāoguò'.

desc: Highest in rank, quality, or degree (adjective)
note: The adjective sense is expressed with a different word from the noun and verb senses.

desc: To surpass or exceed
note: The verb sense uses a distinct word in each language.

desc: A commercial enterprise or company
note: English 'business' covers both a company and commercial activity; German uses 'Geschäft' (company) vs 'Handel' (trade), Japanese 'mise' vs 'shōbai', Mandarin 'shāngdiàn' vs 'shēngyi'.

desc: Commercial activity or trade
note: In phrases like 'do business', the uncountable sense uses a different word from the countable company sense.

desc: To feel concern or interest (verb)
note: English 'care' as a verb has two distinct senses; German 'sich kümmern' for looking after vs 'sich sorgen' for concern; Japanese 'ki ni suru' vs 'sewa o suru'; Mandarin 'zàihu' vs 'zhàogù'.

desc: To look after or tend to someone or something
note: The 'care for' sense uses a different verb from the 'care about' sense in all three languages.

desc: To begin an action or process
note: English 'start' can mean to begin, to activate a machine, or the beginning itself; German 'anfangen' vs 'starten' vs 'Anfang', Japanese 'hajimeru' vs 'sutāto suru' vs 'hajime', Mandarin 'kāishǐ' vs 'qǐdòng' vs 'kāishǐ' (noun).

desc: To cause a machine or engine to begin operating
note: Activating a machine uses a different verb than beginning an activity in German, Japanese, and Mandarin.

desc: The beginning of something (noun)
note: The noun sense is expressed with a different word from the verb senses.

desc: A method, scheme, or organized way of doing something
note: English 'system' can refer to a method or a set of components; German 'Methode' vs 'System', Japanese 'hōhō' vs 'shisutemu', Mandarin 'fāngfǎ' vs 'xìtǒng'.

desc: A set of connected parts forming a complex whole
note: The 'hardware' or 'physical' sense uses a different word from the 'method' sense.

desc: A container or box for holding something
note: English 'case' has distinct meanings: container, situation, and legal proceeding; German 'Koffer' vs 'Fall' vs 'Fall', Japanese 'ケース' vs 'baai' vs 'jiken', Mandarin 'hézi' vs 'qíngkuàng' vs 'ànjiàn'.

desc: A particular situation or instance
note: The situational sense uses a different word from the container sense in each language.

desc: A legal proceeding or lawsuit
note: The legal sense is expressed with a distinct word in German, Japanese, and Mandarin.

desc: An individual human being
note: English 'person' can refer to a human or a grammatical category; German uses 'Person' for both (same), but Japanese uses 'hito' vs 'ninshō', Mandarin 'rén' vs 'rénchēng'.

desc: A grammatical category distinguishing first, second, third person
note: In Japanese and Mandarin, the grammatical sense uses a different word from the human sense.

desc: To make or become different (verb)
note: English 'change' covers altering, exchanging, and the resulting difference; German 'ändern' vs 'wechseln' vs 'Änderung', Japanese 'kawaru' vs 'kaeru' vs 'henka', Mandarin 'gǎibiàn' vs 'huàn' vs 'biànhuà'.

desc: To exchange for something else (verb)
note: The exchange sense uses a different verb from the alteration sense in all three languages.

desc: An instance of being altered or a modification (noun)
note: The noun sense is expressed with a different word from the verb senses.

desc: Containing as much as possible; filled
note: English 'full' can mean filled or satiated after eating; German 'voll' vs 'satt', Japanese 'ippai' (both?) but Mandarin 'mǎn' vs 'bǎo' shows a clear split.

desc: Having eaten enough; no longer hungry
note: In Mandarin, the satiated sense uses 'bǎo' while the filled sense uses 'mǎn'; German and Japanese also differentiate (German 'satt', Japanese 'onaka ga ippai' uses same 'ippai' but construction differs).

desc: taking hold of something with one's hand
note: English uses 'took' for grasping, but German uses 'nehmen', Japanese 'toru', Mandarin 'ná' – all distinct from other meanings.

desc: swallowing medicine or food
note: For ingesting, English still uses 'took', but German uses 'einnehmen', Japanese 'nomu', Mandarin 'chī' – showing a split from physical grasp.

desc: requiring a certain amount of time
note: When expressing time, English uses 'took', while German uses 'dauern', Japanese 'kakaru', Mandarin 'huā' – a completely different verb.

desc: moving towards the speaker or a location
note: The basic sense of 'come' is 'kommen' in German, 'kuru' in Japanese, 'lái' in Mandarin.

desc: becoming true or realized (e.g., prophecy)
note: For 'come true', German uses 'werden', Japanese 'jitsugen suru', Mandarin 'shíxiàn' – all different from arrival.

desc: meeting someone or something unexpectedly (come across)
note: The phrasal verb 'come across' translates to 'begegnen' in German, 'deau' in Japanese, 'yù dào' in Mandarin – each a distinct verb.

desc: a paid position of regular work
note: English 'job' for employment is 'Beruf' or 'Job' in German, 'shigoto' in Japanese, 'gōngzuò' in Mandarin – but for crime, different words appear.

desc: a robbery or heist (slang)
note: In criminal context, German uses 'Raub', Japanese 'gōtō', Mandarin 'qiǎngjié' – showing how the same English word maps to unrelated lexicons.

desc: making an effort to do something
note: For attempting, German uses 'versuchen', Japanese 'yatte miru', Mandarin 'shì' – distinct from tasting.

desc: sampling food or drink to test flavor
note: For tasting, German uses 'probieren', Japanese 'ajimi suru', Mandarin 'cháng' – a different verb in each language.

desc: hearing a case in court
note: For judging a case, German uses 'verhandeln', Japanese 'saiban suru', Mandarin 'shěnpàn' – completely separate from other meanings.

desc: travelling to a place
note: Basic 'go' is 'gehen' in German, 'iku' in Japanese, 'qù' in Mandarin – all distinct from other frames.

desc: becoming a different condition (e.g., went crazy, went sour)
note: For state changes, German uses 'werden', Japanese 'naru' or 'kuruu', Mandarin 'biàn' or 'fā' – different from motion.

desc: sounding or exploding (go off)
note: The phrasal verb 'go off' translates to 'losgehen' in German, 'naru' in Japanese, 'xiǎng' in Mandarin – each a distinct verb.

desc: a long thin mark on a surface
note: For a drawn line, German uses 'Linie', Japanese 'sen', Mandarin 'xiàn' – all different from queue.

desc: a line of people waiting
note: For a queue, German uses 'Schlange', Japanese 'retsu' or 'gyōretsu', Mandarin 'duì' – a separate word.

desc: a row of words in writing
note: For a line of text, German uses 'Zeile', Japanese 'gyō', Mandarin 'háng' – yet another distinct word.

desc: an instruction given by authority
note: For a command, German uses 'Befehl', Japanese 'meirei', Mandarin 'mìnglìng' – distinct from sequence.

desc: arrangement in a particular sequence
note: For order as sequence, German uses 'Reihenfolge', Japanese 'junjo', Mandarin 'shùnxù' – a different word.

desc: a request for goods or services (e.g., placed an order)
note: For placing an order, German uses 'Bestellung', Japanese 'chūmon', Mandarin 'dìngdān' – showing the semantic split.

desc: a social event with celebration
note: For a party, German uses 'Party' or 'Feier', Japanese 'pātī', Mandarin 'pàiduì' – all loanwords or specific.

desc: an organized group of politicians
note: For political party, German uses 'Partei', Japanese 'seitō', Mandarin 'zhèngdǎng' – entirely different from social.

desc: a group organized to search for someone
note: For search party, German uses 'Suchmannschaft', Japanese 'sōsakutai', Mandarin 'sōusuǒ duì' – distinct from other party meanings.

desc: moving quickly on foot
note: For running, German uses 'laufen', Japanese 'hashiru', Mandarin 'pǎo' – distinct from managing.

desc: to be in charge of a business or organization
note: For managing, German uses 'leiten', Japanese 'keiei suru', Mandarin 'jīngyíng' – a completely different verb.

desc: to flow or move freely (e.g., water runs)
note: For liquid flow, German uses 'fließen', Japanese 'nagareru', Mandarin 'liú' – each a unique verb.

desc: help or support provided to customers
note: For customer service, German uses 'Service', Japanese 'sābisu', Mandarin 'fúwù' – often borrowed but distinct from military.

desc: time spent in the armed forces
note: For military service, German uses 'Dienst', Japanese 'heieki', Mandarin 'bīngyì' – a different word.

desc: regular upkeep of equipment (e.g., car service)
note: For maintenance, German uses 'Wartung', Japanese 'mentenansu', Mandarin 'bǎoyǎng' – yet another distinct word.

desc: a sovereign state
note: For a nation, German uses 'Land', Japanese 'kuni', Mandarin 'guójiā' – distinct from homeland.

desc: one's native country
note: For homeland, German uses 'Heimat', Japanese 'sokoku', Mandarin 'zǔguó' – showing a different word from nation.

desc: to cause a door to become open
note: For opening a door, German uses 'öffnen', Japanese 'akeru', Mandarin 'dǎkāi' – distinct from opening a business.

desc: to start a new business or store
note: For commencing a business, German uses 'eröffnen', Japanese 'kaiten suru', Mandarin 'kāizhāng' – a different verb.

desc: one of the four periods of the year
note: For a season of the year, German uses 'Jahreszeit', Japanese 'kisetsu', Mandarin 'jìjié' – distinct from sports season.

desc: a period when a particular sport is played
note: For a sports season, German uses 'Saison', Japanese 'shīzun', Mandarin 'sàijì' – each a different word.

desc: a high-ranking officer in the armed forces
note: For a general officer, German uses 'General', Japanese 'taishō' or 'shōgun', Mandarin 'jiāngjūn' – all different from the adjective sense.

desc: employing a tool or object for a purpose
note: For using a tool, German uses 'benutzen', Japanese 'tsukau', Mandarin 'yòng' – distinct from consuming.

desc: depleting a resource completely
note: For using up, German uses 'verbrauchen', Japanese 'tsukai kiru', Mandarin 'yòng wán' – a different verb.

desc: taking unfair advantage of someone
note: For exploiting someone, German uses 'ausnutzen', Japanese 'riyō suru', Mandarin 'lìyòng' – yet another distinct verb.

desc: Relating to a particular place or area.
note: German, Japanese, and Mandarin use a different word for this adjective sense than for the noun sense 'a local person'.

desc: A person who lives in a particular area.
note: This noun sense uses distinct words in German ('Einheimischer'), Japanese ('地元の人'), and Mandarin ('当地人') compared to the adjective sense.

desc: A long, sturdy piece of wood or metal set upright.
note: German uses 'Pfosten', Japanese uses '柱', Mandarin uses '柱子' – all different from the verb sense 'to mail'.

desc: To send a letter or package by mail.
note: German uses 'aufgeben' or 'einwerfen', Japanese uses '出す', Mandarin uses '寄' – contrasting with the noun sense for a pole.

desc: A job or position.
note: German uses 'Stelle', Japanese uses 'ポスト' or '役職', Mandarin uses '职位' – distinct from both the pole and mail senses.

desc: A recording of moving images.
note: German uses 'Video', Japanese uses 'ビデオ', Mandarin uses '视频' – these differ from the verb sense 'to record'.

desc: To record with a video camera.
note: German uses 'filmen', Japanese uses '撮る' or '録画する', Mandarin uses '拍摄' – contrasting with the noun sense.

desc: Having lived for only a short time; not old.
note: German uses 'jung', Japanese uses '若い', Mandarin uses '年轻的' – these differ from the noun sense 'offspring'.

desc: Offspring, especially of animals.
note: German uses 'Junge', Japanese uses '子' or '幼獣', Mandarin uses '幼仔' – contrasting with the adjective sense.

desc: To speak in order to give information or express ideas.
note: German uses 'sprechen', Japanese uses '話す', Mandarin uses '说话' – these differ from the noun sense 'a conversation'.

desc: A conversation or discussion.
note: German uses 'Gespräch', Japanese uses '話' or '会話', Mandarin uses '谈话' – distinct from the verb sense and from 'a lecture'.

desc: A formal lecture or presentation.
note: German uses 'Vortrag', Japanese uses '講演', Mandarin uses '演讲' – a separate noun sense from 'conversation'.

desc: Past participle of 'give'; something that has been handed over or provided.
note: German uses 'gegeben', Japanese uses '与えられた', Mandarin uses '给了' – contrasting with the noun sense 'an assumption'.

desc: An established or assumed fact or situation.
note: German uses 'Gegebenheit', Japanese uses '前提', Mandarin uses '既定事实' – distinct from the past participle.

desc: Either of two equal or corresponding parts into which something is divided.
note: German uses 'Hälfte', Japanese uses '半分', Mandarin uses '一半' – these differ from the adjective sense 'being one of two equal parts'.

desc: Being one of two equal parts; amounting to half.
note: German uses 'halb', Japanese uses '半', Mandarin uses '半' – distinct from the noun sense, e.g., 'a half' vs 'half an hour'.

desc: The end part of the arm beyond the wrist.
note: German uses 'Hand', Japanese uses '手', Mandarin uses '手' – contrasting with the verb sense 'to pass' and the noun sense 'help'.

desc: To give or pass something to someone.
note: German uses 'reichen' or 'geben', Japanese uses '渡す', Mandarin uses '递给' – distinct from the body part noun.

desc: Help or assistance, especially in a task.
note: German uses 'Hilfe', Japanese uses '助け', Mandarin uses '帮助' – a different noun sense from the body part.

desc: A position on a scale of amount, quality, or degree.
note: German uses 'Niveau', Japanese uses 'レベル', Mandarin uses '水平' – distinct from the verb sense 'to make flat' and the adjective sense 'flat'.

desc: To make something flat or even.
note: German uses 'ebnen', Japanese uses '平らにする', Mandarin uses '使平坦' – contrasting with the noun sense.

desc: Having a flat, even surface; not sloping.
note: German uses 'eben', Japanese uses '平らな', Mandarin uses '平坦的' – distinct from the noun and verb senses.

desc: The faculty of consciousness, thought, and memory.
note: German uses 'Geist', Japanese uses '心' or '精神', Mandarin uses '思想' – different from the verb sense 'to be careful of'.

desc: To be careful of or pay attention to something.
note: German uses 'aufpassen' or 'achten', Japanese uses '気をつける', Mandarin uses '注意' – contrasting with the noun sense.

desc: Not married or in a romantic relationship.
note: German uses 'ledig' or 'single', Japanese uses '独身の', Mandarin uses '单身的' – distinct from the verb sense 'to select' and the noun sense 'a single person'.

desc: An unmarried person (often in classifieds).
note: German uses 'Single', Japanese uses '独身者', Mandarin uses '单身人士' – contrasting with the adjective and verb senses.

desc: To choose or pick out from a group.
note: German uses 'herausgreifen', Japanese uses '選び出す', Mandarin uses '挑选' – distinct from the adjective and noun senses.

desc: To change into a specific role or thing (e.g., become a teacher).
note: Mandarin uses '成为' for this sense, while for becoming a state it uses '变得' – English uses one verb for both.

desc: To change into a specific state or quality (e.g., become angry).
note: Mandarin uses '变得' here, contrasting with '成为' for roles – German and Japanese use the same verb ('werden' / 'なる') for both.

desc: The physical structure of a person or animal.
note: German uses 'Körper', Japanese uses '体', Mandarin uses '身体' – distinct from the sense 'corpse' and 'main part of a vehicle'.

desc: A dead body; a corpse.
note: German uses 'Leiche', Japanese uses '死体', Mandarin uses '尸体' – a different word from the living body sense.

desc: The main or central part of something, especially a vehicle.
note: German uses 'Karosserie' (car), Japanese uses '車体', Mandarin uses '车身' – contrasting with the human body and corpse senses.

desc: Present participle of 'come'; arriving or approaching.
note: German uses 'kommend' as a participle, but the noun sense uses 'Ankunft' – Japanese and Mandarin also use different words.

desc: An arrival or approach (e.g., the coming of spring).
note: German uses 'Ankunft', Japanese uses '到来', Mandarin uses '来临' – distinct from the participle and adjective uses.

desc: Future or approaching (e.g., the coming week).
note: German uses 'kommend' (same as participle but adjectival), Japanese uses '来る' (as attributive), Mandarin uses '即将到来的' – a separate frame from the noun.

desc: The power to influence or direct behavior or events.
note: German uses 'Kontrolle', Japanese uses '支配' or '制御', Mandarin uses '控制' – distinct from the verb and 'device' senses.

desc: A device or mechanism used to operate a machine.
note: German uses 'Bedienelement' or 'Steuerung', Japanese uses '制御装置', Mandarin uses '控制器' – contrasting with the abstract noun.

desc: To have power over or regulate something.
note: German uses 'kontrollieren', Japanese uses '支配する' or '制御する', Mandarin uses '控制' (same as noun) – but German and Japanese use different verb forms.

desc: The end of life of a living being.
note: German uses 'Tod', Japanese uses '死', Mandarin uses '死亡' – contrasting with the metaphorical sense for things like businesses.

desc: The end or destruction of something non‑living (e.g., death of a company).
note: German uses 'Untergang', Japanese uses '終焉', Mandarin uses '灭亡' – a different word from literal death.

desc: Edible substances in general (e.g., bought at a store).
note: German uses 'Lebensmittel', Japanese uses '食べ物', Mandarin uses '食物' – distinct from the sense 'prepared meal'.

desc: A particular prepared meal or dish (e.g., the food was delicious).
note: German uses 'Essen', Japanese uses '食事', Mandarin uses '饭菜' – contrasting with the general substance sense.

desc: A period of instruction in a subject.
note: German uses 'Unterricht', Japanese uses '授業', Mandarin uses '课' for the lesson, while for social class they use 'Klasse', '階級', '阶级'.

desc: A division of society based on social or economic status.
note: German 'Klasse', Japanese '階級', Mandarin '阶级' contrast with the lesson frame using different words.

desc: To shut something.
note: German 'schließen', Japanese '閉める', Mandarin '关' differ from the adjective meaning 'near'.

desc: Being near in distance.
note: German 'nah', Japanese '近い', Mandarin '近' contrast with the verb 'shut'.

desc: A reason or explanation for something.
note: German 'Ursache', Japanese '原因', Mandarin '原因' contrast with the verb 'to cause'.

desc: To make something happen or be the reason for it.
note: German 'verursachen', Japanese '引き起こす', Mandarin '导致' differ from the noun 'cause'.

desc: Feeling joy about a specific event or situation.
note: German 'froh' or 'glücklich', Japanese '嬉しい', Mandarin '高兴' contrast with the general life satisfaction frame.

desc: Being content with one's life overall.
note: German 'glücklich', Japanese '幸せ', Mandarin '幸福' differ from the transient joy frame.

desc: A person, as opposed to an animal or object.
note: German 'Mensch', Japanese '人間', Mandarin '人' contrast with the adjective for 'relating to people'.

desc: Relating to or characteristic of people.
note: German 'menschlich', Japanese '人間の', Mandarin '人类的' differ from the noun 'person'.

desc: To change physical position or location.
note: German 'bewegen', Japanese '動く/動かす', Mandarin '移动' contrast with the noun 'move in a game'.

desc: A turn or action in a game, especially chess.
note: German 'Zug', Japanese '手', Mandarin '步' differ from the verb 'move'.

desc: A sentence or phrase that asks for information.
note: German 'Frage', Japanese '質問', Mandarin '问题' contrast with the verb 'to ask'.

desc: To ask someone about something.
note: German 'fragen', Japanese '質問する/問う', Mandarin '问' differ from the noun 'question'.

desc: A set of related television programs broadcast in order.
note: German 'Serie', Japanese 'シリーズ', Mandarin '连续剧' contrast with a general sequence.

desc: A number of events or items occurring one after another.
note: German 'Reihe', Japanese '連続', Mandarin '系列' differ from the TV series frame.

desc: To stay in a place until something expected happens.
note: German 'warten', Japanese '待つ', Mandarin '等/等待' contrast with the meaning 'to serve'.

desc: To serve food or drink to someone at a table.
note: German 'bedienen', Japanese '給仕する', Mandarin '服务' differ from 'to wait for'.

desc: To put a question to someone in order to get information.
note: German 'fragen', Japanese '聞く/尋ねる', Mandarin '问' contrast with the meaning 'to request'.

desc: To request something from someone.
note: German 'bitten', Japanese '頼む', Mandarin '请求' differ from 'to inquire'.

desc: A group of people living in the same place or having a particular characteristic in common.
note: German 'Gemeinschaft', Japanese '地域社会', Mandarin '社区' contrast with a community of shared interest.

desc: A group of people with a common interest or goal, not necessarily geographic.
note: German 'Gemeinschaft' (same word), Japanese 'コミュニティ/共同体', Mandarin '社群' differ from the geographic frame in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: After the expected or usual time.
note: German 'spät', Japanese '遅い', Mandarin '晚' contrast with the meaning 'deceased'.

desc: No longer alive; deceased (used before a noun).
note: German 'verstorben', Japanese '亡き', Mandarin '已故' differ from 'tardy'.

desc: To go away from a place or person.
note: German 'verlassen', Japanese '去る', Mandarin '离开' contrast with 'to allow to remain'.

desc: To cause something to remain or stay in place.
note: German 'lassen', Japanese '残す', Mandarin '留下' differ from 'to depart'.

desc: The direction that is opposite south.
note: German 'Norden', Japanese '北', Mandarin '北' contrast with the adjective meaning 'located in the north'.

desc: Situated in or towards the north.
note: German 'nördlich', Japanese '北の', Mandarin '北方的' differ from the noun 'north'.

desc: A tool with a serrated blade used for cutting hard materials.
note: German 'Säge', Japanese 'のこぎり', Mandarin '锯' contrast with the past tense of 'see'.

desc: Past tense of 'see' – perceived with the eyes.
note: German 'sah', Japanese '見た', Mandarin '看见' differ from the tool.

desc: A small timepiece worn on the wrist.
note: German 'Uhr', Japanese '時計', Mandarin '手表' contrast with the verb 'to watch'.

desc: To look at something attentively or observe over time.
note: German 'beobachten/schauen', Japanese '見る/観察する', Mandarin '看/观察' differ from the noun 'watch'.

desc: light (lamp) – a device that produces illumination
note: In German 'Lampe', Japanese 'dentou', Mandarin 'dēng' are distinct from the word for natural illumination.

desc: light (not heavy) – having little weight
note: German 'leicht', Japanese 'karui', Mandarin 'qīng' differ from the word for pale color.

desc: light (pale color) – pale in color
note: German 'hell', Japanese 'usui', Mandarin 'qiǎn' are different from the word for weight.

desc: low (noun meaning a low point or level)
note: German 'Tief', Japanese 'teiten', Mandarin 'dī diǎn' are distinct from the adjective 'low' (niedrig/hikui/dī).

desc: police (verb) – to monitor or control an area
note: German 'überwachen', Japanese 'kanshi suru', Mandarin 'wéichí zhìxù' differ from the noun 'police'.

desc: short (height) – of small vertical extent
note: German 'klein', Japanese 'hikui', Mandarin 'ǎi' are separate from words for length.

desc: short (lack) – insufficient in quantity
note: German 'knapp', Japanese 'tarinai', Mandarin 'quē' differ from height and length words.

desc: stay (remain) – to continue in a place
note: German 'bleiben', Japanese 'iru', Mandarin 'dāi' are used for remaining, distinct from temporary lodging.

desc: stay (temporarily lodge) – to live somewhere short-term
note: German 'übernachten', Japanese 'tomeru', Mandarin 'zhù' differ from the verb 'remain'.

desc: age (era) – a distinct historical period
note: German 'Zeitalter', Japanese 'jidai', Mandarin 'shídài' are different from the verb 'to age'.

desc: age (verb) – to grow old
note: German 'altern', Japanese 'toshi o toru', Mandarin 'biàn lǎo' contrast with the noun meanings.

desc: buy (purchase) – to obtain in exchange for money
note: German 'kaufen', Japanese 'kau', Mandarin 'mǎi' are the basic verb.

desc: buy (accept) – to believe something, often used in 'I don't buy that'
note: German 'glauben', Japanese 'shinjiru', Mandarin 'xiāngxìn' differ from the purchase sense.

desc: buy (bribe) – to pay for illegal influence
note: German 'bestechen', Japanese 'wairo o okuru', Mandarin 'huìlù' are distinct from purchase.

desc: deal (noun agreement) – a business arrangement
note: German 'Geschäft', Japanese 'torihiki', Mandarin 'jiāoyì' are distinct from the verb 'deal with'.

desc: deal (verb handle) – to manage or cope with something
note: German 'umgehen', Japanese 'taisho suru', Mandarin 'chǔlǐ' differ from the noun.

desc: report (loud noise) – a sudden loud sound, e.g., of a gun
note: German 'Knall', Japanese 'hibiki', Mandarin 'shēng' contrast with the document sense.

desc: report (document) – a written or spoken account
note: German 'Bericht', Japanese 'hōkoku', Mandarin 'bàogào' are different from the noise meaning.

desc: third (ordinal) – coming after the second
note: German 'dritter', Japanese 'daisan no', Mandarin 'dìsān' are used for ordinal, distinct from the fraction.

desc: third (fraction) – one of three equal parts
note: German 'Drittel', Japanese 'sanbun no ichi', Mandarin 'sān fēn zhī yī' differ from ordinal.

desc: turn (rotate) – to move in a circular motion
note: German 'drehen', Japanese 'mawasu', Mandarin 'zhuàn' are used for rotation.

desc: turn (become) – to change state or color
note: German 'werden', Japanese 'ni naru', Mandarin 'biàn' contrast with rotation.

desc: turn (opportunity) – a chance to do something in sequence
note: German 'Reihe', Japanese 'junsu', Mandarin 'lún' are different from the verbs.

desc: check (verify) – to examine or test
note: German 'prüfen', Japanese 'chekku suru', Mandarin 'jiǎnchá' are the verb.

desc: check (bank check) – a written order for payment
note: German 'Scheck', Japanese 'kogitte', Mandarin 'zhīpiào' differ from the verb.

desc: check (chess) – a threat to the king
note: German 'Schach', Japanese 'chekku', Mandarin 'shā qí' are distinct from other meanings.

desc: development (housing) – a built area of houses or buildings
note: German 'Wohnsiedlung', Japanese 'kaihatsu', Mandarin 'kāifā qū' differ from the abstract 'development'.

desc: form (shape) – the visible structure of something
note: German 'Form', Japanese 'katachi', Mandarin 'xíngshì' contrast with the document sense.

desc: form (document) – a printed paper with blanks
note: German 'Formular', Japanese 'yōshi', Mandarin 'biǎodān' are different from shape.

desc: heart (organ) – the muscular organ that pumps blood
note: German 'Herz', Japanese 'shinzō', Mandarin 'xīnzàng' are the medical term, distinct from 'center'.

desc: heart (center) – the innermost part or core
note: German 'Zentrum', Japanese 'chūshin', Mandarin 'zhōngxīn' differ from organ and emotion senses.

desc: act (action) – a deed or performance of a task
note: German 'Handlung', Japanese 'kōi', Mandarin 'xíngdòng' contrast with theatrical act.

desc: act (performance) – a part of a play or show
note: German 'Akt', Japanese 'engeki', Mandarin 'biǎoyǎn' differ from action.

desc: act (verb) – to take action or behave
note: German 'handeln', Japanese 'kōdō suru', Mandarin 'zuò' are distinct from the nouns.

desc: Fire as a substance that burns, producing heat and light.
note: German uses 'Feuer', Japanese '火 (hi)', Mandarin '火 (huǒ)' — all use a different word from the verb forms.

desc: Fire as in to discharge a gun or weapon.
note: German uses 'schießen', Japanese '発砲する (happō suru)', Mandarin '开枪 (kāiqiāng)' — distinct from the noun and from the dismissal sense.

desc: Fire as in to dismiss someone from employment.
note: German 'entlassen', Japanese '解雇する (kaiko suru)', Mandarin '解雇 (jiěgù)' — a different verb from shooting.

desc: Major as a military rank above captain.
note: German 'Major', Japanese '少佐 (shōsa)', Mandarin '少校 (shàoxiào)' — distinct from academic major.

desc: Major as a student's primary area of study.
note: German 'Hauptfach', Japanese '専攻 (senkō)', Mandarin '专业 (zhuānyè)' — a different word from the military rank.

desc: Major as a verb meaning to study a particular subject as one's primary focus.
note: German 'als Hauptfach studieren', Japanese '専攻する (senkō suru)', Mandarin '主修 (zhǔxiū)' — the verb form is distinct from the noun forms.

desc: Phone as a device for voice communication.
note: German 'Telefon', Japanese '電話 (denwa)', Mandarin '电话 (diànhuà)' — the noun is a different word from the verb.

desc: Phone as a verb meaning to call someone.
note: German 'anrufen', Japanese '電話する (denwa suru)', Mandarin '打电话 (dǎ diànhuà)' — the verb uses a different expression than the noun.

desc: Art as creative works like painting, sculpture, music.
note: German 'Kunst', Japanese '芸術 (geijutsu)', Mandarin '艺术 (yìshù)' — distinct from the skill sense.

desc: Art as a skill or craft in a particular activity.
note: German 'Kunstfertigkeit' or 'Technik', Japanese '技術 (gijutsu)', Mandarin '技艺 (jìyì)' — a different word from fine arts.

desc: Building as a physical structure like a house or office.
note: German 'Gebäude', Japanese '建物 (tatemono)', Mandarin '建筑 (jiànzhù)' — the noun is distinct from the verb form.

desc: Building as the present participle of 'build', meaning constructing.
note: German uses 'bauend' or 'am Bauen', Japanese '建てている (tatete iru)', Mandarin '正在建 (zhèngzài jiàn)' — a different word form from the noun.

desc: Market as a place where goods are bought and sold.
note: German 'Markt', Japanese '市場 (ichiba)', Mandarin '市场 (shìchǎng)' — the noun is a different word from the verb.

desc: Market as a verb meaning to promote or sell a product.
note: German 'vermarkten', Japanese '販売する (hanbai suru)' or 'マーケティングする', Mandarin '营销 (yíngxiāo)' — the verb uses a different word.

desc: Plan as a detailed proposal for doing something.
note: German 'Plan', Japanese '計画 (keikaku)', Mandarin '计划 (jìhuà)' — the noun is often the same word as the verb in Mandarin, but German and Japanese use different verb forms.

desc: Plan as a verb meaning to decide on a course of action.
note: German 'planen', Japanese '計画する (keikaku suru)', Mandarin '计划 (jìhuà)' (same character but different part of speech) — this verb contrasts with the noun in German and Japanese.

desc: Final as a noun meaning the last exam or competition in a series.
note: German 'Finale' or 'Abschlussprüfung', Japanese '決勝 (kesshō)' or '期末試験', Mandarin '决赛 (juésài)' or '期末考试' — the noun is a different word from the adjective 'final'.

desc: Front as the forward-facing surface or side of something.
note: German 'Vorderseite', Japanese '正面 (shōmen)', Mandarin '前面 (qiánmiàn)' — distinct from other noun senses.

desc: Front as a false appearance or cover for illegal activity.
note: German 'Deckmantel' or 'Fassade', Japanese '隠れ蓑 (kakuremino)' or '表向き', Mandarin '幌子 (huǎngzi)' — a different word from spatial front.

desc: Front as a boundary between air masses in meteorology.
note: German 'Front', Japanese '前線 (zensen)', Mandarin '锋 (fēng)' — a specialized noun distinct from others.

desc: Front as a verb meaning to act as a front or to pretend.
note: German 'vorspielen' or 'als Deckmantel dienen', Japanese 'ふりをする (furi o suru)', Mandarin '假装 (jiǎzhuāng)' — the verb is a different word from nouns.

desc: List as a series of items written down.
note: German 'Liste', Japanese 'リスト (risuto)', Mandarin '列表 (lièbiǎo)' — the noun is a different word from the verb.

desc: List as a verb meaning to write or mention items one by one.
note: German 'auflisten', Japanese 'リストアップする (risuto appu suru)', Mandarin '列出 (lièchū)' — the verb uses a different word.

desc: Ready as a verb meaning to make preparations or get something prepared.
note: German 'bereitmachen', Japanese '準備する (junbi suru)', Mandarin '准备 (zhǔnbèi)' — the verb is distinct from the more common adjective 'ready'.

desc: A physical room or building where people work.
note: English uses 'office' for both the place and the position, but German 'Büro' vs 'Amt', Japanese '事務所' vs '役職', and Mandarin '办公室' vs '职位' distinguish them.

desc: An official position or role, especially in government or an organization.
note: This frame contrasts with the physical room; in German, 'Amt' is used for position, while 'Büro' is for the room.

desc: To give money in exchange for goods or services.
note: English uses 'pay' for financial transactions and for suffering consequences, but German 'bezahlen' vs 'büßen', Japanese '払う' vs '償う', and Mandarin '付' vs '付出代价' separate them.

desc: To suffer or be punished for a mistake or wrongdoing.
note: This metaphorical use of 'pay' is distinct from monetary payment; other languages often have a specific verb for atonement.

desc: The cardinal direction opposite north.
note: English uses 'south' for both the direction and the region, but Japanese '南' vs '南部' and Mandarin '南' vs '南方' distinguish them; German uses 'Süden' for both.

desc: A geographical region located toward the south, often with cultural or political significance.
note: This frame refers to a specific area, e.g., 'the South' of a country, which is lexicalized differently in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: In accordance with fact or reality; not false.
note: English uses 'true' for both factual accuracy and authenticity, but German 'wahr' vs 'echt', Japanese '真実の' vs '本当の', and Mandarin '真实的' vs '真正的' separate them.

desc: Genuine, authentic, or having the essential qualities of something.
note: This frame highlights the difference between factual truth and genuine nature, which languages like Japanese and Mandarin lexicalize differently.

desc: Recognized as a fact or generally acknowledged.
note: English uses 'known' for both factual awareness and fame, but Japanese '知られている' vs '有名な' and Mandarin '已知的' vs '著名的' distinguish them.

desc: Widely known; famous or well-known.
note: This frame contrasts with mere factual knowledge; it implies reputation and is expressed with different words in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: Big in physical size or dimensions.
note: English uses 'large' for both physical size and quantity, but Japanese '大きい' vs '大量の' and Mandarin '大的' vs '大量的' separate them; German 'groß' covers both.

desc: Great in amount, number, or extent.
note: This frame refers to magnitude of quantity, which is expressed with a different adjective in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: Unable to find one's way; disoriented.
note: English uses 'lost' for both geographical disorientation and losing a competition, but German 'verirrt' vs 'verloren', Japanese '迷子' vs '敗北した', and Mandarin '迷路' vs '失败的' distinguish them.

desc: Not won; having been defeated or unsuccessful.
note: This frame contrasts with physical loss of direction; in many languages, a different adjective is used for a lost game or battle.

desc: A part of a building enclosed by walls, floor, and ceiling.
note: English uses 'room' for both a discrete space and available space, but German 'Zimmer' vs 'Platz', Japanese '部屋' vs 'スペース', and Mandarin '房间' vs '空间' distinguish them.

desc: Available or sufficient space, especially for a purpose.
note: This uncountable sense of 'room' refers to capacity, and is expressed with a different noun in many languages.

desc: To be victorious in a competition, game, or conflict.
note: English uses 'win' for both defeating opponents and acquiring a prize, but Japanese '勝つ' vs '獲得する' and Mandarin '赢' vs '获得' separate them; German 'gewinnen' can cover both.

desc: To gain or receive something as a result of effort or success.
note: This frame emphasizes acquiring a prize or reward, which is expressed with a different verb in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: Not correct; based on error or misunderstanding.
note: English uses 'wrong' for both factual error and moral transgression, but Japanese '間違った' vs '悪い' and Mandarin '错误' vs '不道德' distinguish them; German 'falsch' can cover both.

desc: Morally or ethically unjust; not right.
note: This frame separates factual incorrectness from moral wrongness, which are lexicalized differently in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: A female child or young woman.
note: English uses 'girl' for both a female child and one's daughter, but German 'Mädchen' vs 'Tochter', Japanese '女の子' vs '娘', and Mandarin '女孩' vs '女儿' distinguish them.

desc: A female offspring; one's daughter.
note: This frame specifically refers to a child in relation to parents, which is expressed with a separate word in many languages.

desc: A subject, situation, or affair under consideration.
note: English uses 'matter' as a noun for an issue and as a verb for importance, but German 'Sache' vs 'wichtig sein', Japanese '問題' vs '重要である', and Mandarin '事情' vs '要紧' separate them.

desc: To be of importance or significance.
note: This verb frame contrasts with the noun; in many languages, a completely different expression is used for 'it matters'.

desc: Attractive in a delicate or graceful way.
note: English uses 'pretty' as both an adjective meaning attractive and an adverb meaning fairly, but German 'hübsch' vs 'ziemlich', Japanese 'かわいい' vs 'かなり', and Mandarin '漂亮' vs '相当' distinguish them.

desc: To a moderately high degree; fairly.
note: This adverbial use of 'pretty' is completely different in meaning and is expressed with a separate word in many languages.

desc: The invisible gaseous substance surrounding the earth.
note: English uses 'air' for both the gas and broadcasting, but German 'Luft' vs 'ausstrahlen', Japanese '空気' vs '放送する', and Mandarin '空气' vs '播出' separate them.

desc: To broadcast a program on television or radio.
note: This verb meaning is derived from the noun but uses a completely different word in other languages.

desc: A small piece or part of something.
note: English uses 'bit' for both a small piece and a small degree, but German 'Stück' vs 'bisschen', Japanese '一片' vs '少し', and Mandarin '一块' vs '一点' distinguish them.

desc: A small amount or degree; used adverbially to modify adjectives or verbs.
note: This quantifier use of 'a bit' is lexicalized differently in many languages, often as an adverb rather than a noun.

desc: A person with whom one has a bond of mutual affection.
note: English uses 'friend' for both a close acquaintance and a supporter of a cause, but Japanese '友達' vs '味方' and Mandarin '朋友' vs '支持者' distinguish them; German 'Freund' can cover both.

desc: A person who supports a particular cause, organization, or idea.
note: This frame refers to an ally or advocate, which is expressed with a different word in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: To strike or come into contact with forcefully.
note: English uses 'hit' as both a verb for striking and a noun for a popular success, but German 'schlagen' vs 'Hit', Japanese '打つ' vs 'ヒット', and Mandarin '打' vs '热门' separate them.

desc: A popular success, especially in entertainment or business.
note: This noun frame is a borrowing in many languages but is distinct from the verb; it reveals how English uses the same word for two different concepts.

desc: Pleasant; enjoyable or satisfactory.
note: English uses 'nice' for both pleasant and kind, but German 'schön' vs 'nett', Japanese 'いい' (ambiguous) but also '親切' for kind, and Mandarin '好' (ambiguous) but also '友善' for kind show some distinction.

desc: Kind, friendly, or considerate in manner.
note: This frame emphasizes personal character, which is often expressed with a different adjective in German and sometimes in Japanese and Mandarin.

desc: To grasp the meaning or logic of something intellectually.
note: English uses 'understand' for both intellectual comprehension and emotional empathy, but German 'verstehen' vs 'nachfühlen', Japanese 'わかる' vs '共感する', and Mandarin '理解' vs '同情' distinguish them.

desc: To empathize with someone's feelings or situation; to appreciate emotionally.
note: This frame separates cognitive understanding from emotional empathy, which are lexicalized as different verbs in many languages.

desc: A flat piece of wood or other rigid material.
note: English 'board' as a plank differs from committee sense; German 'Brett', Japanese '板', Mandarin '板' contrast with 'Vorstand', '取締役会', '董事会'.

desc: A group of people who jointly manage an organization.
note: Committee sense uses different words in German, Japanese, and Mandarin compared to the plank sense.

desc: To get onto a vehicle such as a train, ship, or aircraft.
note: Verb 'board' translates to German 'an Bord gehen', Japanese '乗る', Mandarin '上', all distinct from the noun senses.

desc: The amount of money needed to buy or do something.
note: English noun 'cost' corresponds to German 'Kosten', Japanese '費用', Mandarin '成本'; the verb 'cost' uses separate roots like 'kosten', 'かかる', '花费'.

desc: To require a particular payment.
note: Verb 'cost' is distinct from the noun in all three target languages, revealing a common noun/verb split.

desc: To divide or separate with a sharp instrument.
note: Physical cutting uses German 'schneiden', Japanese '切る', Mandarin '切'; reduction uses different verbs like 'kürzen', '削減する', '削减'.

desc: To reduce the amount or size of something.
note: Reduction meaning is lexically distinct from physical cutting in all three languages.

desc: A wound or opening made by cutting.
note: Noun 'cut' (injury) translates to German 'Schnitt', Japanese '傷', Mandarin '伤口', contrasting with the verb senses.

desc: An area of open land, especially used for farming or sports.
note: Agricultural 'field' uses German 'Feld', Japanese '畑', Mandarin '田地'; academic 'field' uses 'Gebiet', '分野', '领域'—a clear lexical split.

desc: A particular domain of knowledge or activity.
note: Academic field sense is expressed with different words than the agricultural sense in all three languages.

desc: To handle or respond to a question or situation.
note: Verb 'field' (as in field questions) uses German 'abfangen', Japanese '受け答える', Mandarin '回答', distinct from the nouns.

desc: A principal pipe or cable for water, gas, or electricity.
note: The noun 'main' (e.g., water main) translates to German 'Hauptleitung', Japanese '本管', Mandarin '主管'; the adjective 'main' uses 'Haupt-', '主要な', '主要', so they are different word classes across languages.

desc: A female parent.
note: Noun 'mother' is German 'Mutter', Japanese '母', Mandarin '母亲'; the verb 'to mother' uses separate words like 'bemuttern', '世話する', '照顾'.

desc: To care for someone in a protective manner.
note: Verb 'mother' is lexically different from the noun in all three target languages.

desc: To engage in physical combat with someone.
note: Physical fighting uses German 'kämpfen', Japanese '戦う', Mandarin '打斗'; verbal arguing uses distinct verbs: 'streiten', '喧嘩する', '争吵'.

desc: To argue or quarrel with someone.
